title: The case for studying things that won't earn you money
summary: Argue whether subjects with no direct commercial application — philosophy, classical languages, art history — are worth studying, and what is lost when education is reduced to workforce preparation.
topic: The case for studying things that won't earn you money
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether subjects with no direct commercial value — philosophy, classics, art history — are worth studying. Refer to at least one concrete example (e.g. the decline of Liberal Arts programmes at US universities under funding pressure, or the contrast between US liberal arts tracks and European vocational education streams). Use a cleft sentence, a concession, a participle clause, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What the steady defunding of humanities departments at US land-grant universities reveals is not a shift in student preference but…
Granted, the argument that education should produce employable graduates is not without…
Having measured the value of a degree almost entirely in starting salary, university rankings have…
The contrast between the US liberal arts tradition and European vocational streams is often misread as…

giáo dục nghề nghiệp

hintVocab.phrase: to be trained to execute but not to question
hintVocab.meaningVi: được đào tạo để thực thi nhưng không phải để đặt câu hỏi
đại học được cấp đất — loại trường đại học công lập của Mỹ nhận đất từ chính phủ liên bang

sampleText:
Between 2012 and 2022, the number of students majoring in English at US universities fell by nearly thirty per cent. Over the same period, philosophy and history departments at several large state institutions were merged, restructured, or quietly closed. What this pattern reflects is not a generation's sudden indifference to ideas but a funding architecture that has made it rational to choose a subject by its salary projections rather than by its questions.

Having measured the value of a degree almost entirely in starting salary, university rankings and government loan schemes have reshaped what students feel permitted to study. A student who chooses classics or art history is now routinely asked to justify that choice in economic terms — a pressure that is rarely applied to engineering or finance. The result is a curriculum that is increasingly optimised for a labour market that, paradoxically, routinely complains that its graduates cannot write clearly, argue coherently, or identify when they are being misled.

Granted, the case for vocational alignment has genuine force. In societies where student debt is substantial and graduate underemployment is a documented problem, it is not unreasonable to ask whether a degree is likely to produce a livelihood. European countries with well-funded vocational tracks — Germany's 'Ausbildung' system, for instance — produce workers who are highly skilled and whose training is respected rather than stigmatised. The argument is not that all education should be non-practical, but that practicality should not be the only measure.

Even so, the case for studying things that cannot be immediately monetised rests on something more durable than nostalgia. Philosophy teaches the habit of examining assumptions; rhetoric teaches the art of constructing an argument that can withstand scrutiny; history teaches that what seems inevitable usually wasn't. None of these skills appears on a job listing, and all of them are deployed every time a manager chooses a strategy, a lawyer reads a contract, or a citizen votes. Graduates trained to execute but not to question are precisely the kind of workforce that makes organisations fragile.

What is at stake is not the survival of any particular department but the idea that a person can be educated — not merely trained. The difference, though it resists being measured in salary surveys, is the difference between someone who can do a job and someone who can ask whether it should be done at all.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft to expose a hidden cause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What this pattern reflects is not a generation's sudden indifference to ideas but a funding architecture' — cấu trúc what-cleft bác bỏ một giải thích sai trước ('không phải sự thờ ơ') rồi đưa ra nguyên nhân thực sự ('a funding architecture') ở cuối câu, nơi nó nổi bật nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What this pattern reflects is not … but …' uses a what-cleft to reject a false explanation before presenting the real cause at sentence-final position, where it receives the greatest emphasis.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Perfect participle clause for compressed reasoning
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having measured the value of a degree almost entirely in starting salary, university rankings … have reshaped …' — cụm phân từ hoàn thành nén nguyên nhân vào đầu câu, tránh phải dùng một mệnh đề 'Because … have measured …' dài hơn và ít trang trọng hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having measured the value of a degree almost entirely in starting salary, university rankings … have reshaped …' compresses a causal clause into a perfect participle, raising the register and eliminating the need for a full 'Because …' clause.

sampleTranslationVi:
Giữa năm 2012 và 2022, số sinh viên chuyên ngành tiếng Anh tại các trường đại học Mỹ giảm gần ba mươi phần trăm. Cùng thời gian đó, các khoa triết học và lịch sử tại một số trường công lập lớn bị sáp nhập, tái cơ cấu hoặc âm thầm đóng cửa. Điều mà mô hình này phản ánh không phải là sự thờ ơ đột ngột của một thế hệ với các ý tưởng mà là một cơ cấu tài trợ đã khiến việc chọn môn học theo dự báo lương hơn là theo câu hỏi của nó trở nên hợp lý.

Sau khi đã đo giá trị của một tấm bằng gần như hoàn toàn bằng mức lương khởi điểm, các bảng xếp hạng đại học và chương trình cho vay của chính phủ đã định hình lại những gì sinh viên cảm thấy được phép học. Một sinh viên chọn cổ điển học hoặc lịch sử nghệ thuật giờ đây thường xuyên được yêu cầu biện minh cho sự lựa chọn đó bằng các thuật ngữ kinh tế — một áp lực hiếm khi được áp dụng cho kỹ thuật hoặc tài chính. Kết quả là một chương trình học ngày càng được tối ưu hóa cho một thị trường lao động mà, nghịch lý thay, thường xuyên phàn nàn rằng sinh viên tốt nghiệp không thể viết rõ ràng, lập luận mạch lạc, hoặc nhận ra khi nào họ đang bị đánh lừa.

Công nhận, lập luận cho sự phù hợp với nghề nghiệp có sức mạnh thực sự. Ở những xã hội nơi nợ sinh viên là đáng kể và tình trạng thất nghiệp dưới mức của sinh viên tốt nghiệp là một vấn đề được ghi chép lại, không phải là không hợp lý khi hỏi liệu một tấm bằng có khả năng tạo ra sinh kế hay không. Các quốc gia châu Âu với các chương trình đào tạo nghề được tài trợ tốt — hệ thống 'Ausbildung' của Đức chẳng hạn — tạo ra những công nhân có kỹ năng cao và được tôn trọng hơn là bị kỳ thị. Lập luận không phải là tất cả giáo dục nên là phi thực tế, mà là tính thực tế không nên là thước đo duy nhất.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận cho việc học những thứ không thể được kiếm tiền ngay lập tức dựa trên điều gì đó bền vững hơn là hoài niệm. Triết học dạy thói quen kiểm tra các giả định; tu từ học dạy nghệ thuật xây dựng một lập luận có thể chịu đựng được sự xem xét kỹ lưỡng; lịch sử dạy rằng những gì có vẻ không thể tránh khỏi thường là không phải như vậy. Không có kỹ năng nào trong số này xuất hiện trên một danh sách công việc, và tất cả chúng đều được áp dụng mỗi khi một nhà quản lý chọn chiến lược, một luật sư đọc hợp đồng, hoặc một công dân bỏ phiếu. Sinh viên tốt nghiệp được đào tạo để thực thi nhưng không phải để đặt câu hỏi chính xác là loại lực lượng lao động khiến các tổ chức trở nên dễ vỡ.

Điều đang bị đe dọa không phải là sự tồn tại của bất kỳ khoa nào cụ thể mà là ý tưởng rằng một người có thể được giáo dục — không chỉ đơn giản là được đào tạo. Sự khác biệt, dù khó có thể đo lường trong các khảo sát lương bổng, là sự khác biệt giữa người có thể làm một công việc và người có thể hỏi liệu nó có nên được làm hay không.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to reject a false explanation before presenting the real cause?
options: What this pattern reflects is not a generation's sudden indifference to ideas but a funding architecture.
This pattern is reflecting what a generation's indifference to ideas and a funding architecture.
It is a funding architecture that this pattern of generation's indifference reflects.
What reflects this pattern is generation's indifference not a funding architecture.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What this pattern reflects is not X but Y' uses a what-cleft with 'not … but …' to first dismiss a false cause then present the real one.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What X reflects is not … but …' structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a perfect participle clause to compress a causal explanation?
options: Having measured the value of a degree almost entirely in starting salary, university rankings have reshaped what students feel permitted to study.
University rankings having measured the degree value, what students feel permitted to study was reshaped.
Measuring the degree value in salary, university rankings has reshaped studying permission.
Being measured by salary, university rankings are reshaping by having studied.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having measured …, university rankings have reshaped …' is a perfect participle clause expressing a prior cause; the subject of the participle matches the subject of the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Perfect participle: 'Having + past participle', shared subject with the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what does the labour market paradoxically complain about?
options: That graduates have studied too many vocational subjects
That graduates cannot write clearly, argue coherently, or identify when they are being misled
That graduates earn too much money for the work they do
That graduates are unwilling to learn new technical skills
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay notes that the labour market 'routinely complains that its graduates cannot write clearly, argue coherently, or identify when they are being misled' — skills developed by humanities subjects.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the word 'paradoxically' in the second paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is Germany's 'Ausbildung' system used to illustrate?
options: That vocational education is always inferior to liberal arts
That European countries are better at education than the US
That well-funded vocational tracks can produce skilled, respected workers
That studying philosophy is valued more in Germany than in the US
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses the Ausbildung system as an example of vocational education that is 'highly skilled and respected rather than stigmatised', conceding that vocational alignment has genuine value.
mcQuestions.hint: The example appears in the concession paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction emphasises that pressure is applied to some students but not others?
options: a pressure that is rarely applied to engineering or finance
a pressure that engineering and finance rarely apply
a pressure which rarely engineering or finance applies
a pressure to which rarely applied engineering finance
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A pressure that is rarely applied to …' uses the passive to focus on the experience of the students rather than on who applies the pressure.
mcQuestions.hint: Passive: 'is rarely applied to' — the agent is omitted, putting focus on the affected party.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'practicality should not be the only measure'?
options: Practical subjects should be banned from universities.
Education can have value that goes beyond immediate employability.
Students should not care about getting a job after graduation.
Only impractical subjects produce useful skills.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that while practicality is a valid criterion, reducing education entirely to workforce preparation ignores other kinds of value.
mcQuestions.hint: The word 'only' is key — the argument is about proportion, not total rejection.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means the same as 'intrinsic value' — value that something has in itself?
options: marketable skill
return on investment
something more durable than nostalgia
salary projection
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that the case for studying non-commercial subjects 'rests on something more durable than nostalgia' — implying they have genuine, lasting worth beyond sentimentality.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what the essay says studying non-commercial subjects 'rests on'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay identify as the ultimate difference between being educated and merely trained?
options: Educated people earn more money over a lifetime.
Educated people can ask whether a job should be done at all, not just how to do it.
Trained people make better employees in all sectors.
Education produces creativity; training produces discipline.
mcQuestions.explanation: The final sentence distinguishes 'someone who can do a job' (trained) from 'someone who can ask whether it should be done at all' (educated).
mcQuestions.hint: Read the very last sentence of the essay.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'rather than' to express preference or contrast?
options: respected rather than stigmatised
respected rather stigmatised than
respected than stigmatised rather
rather respected than stigmatised is
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rather than' is placed between the two contrasted elements: 'respected rather than stigmatised'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Rather than' sits between the two contrasting terms.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word in the essay means 'to examine carefully and in detail'?
options: monetise
scrutinise
equip
stigmatise
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Scrutinise' means to examine or inspect closely; the essay uses 'scrutiny' in the phrase 'withstand scrutiny'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the noun form 'scrutiny' in the third-to-last paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'Graduates trained to execute but not to question are precisely the kind of workforce that makes organisations fragile'?
options: Companies want graduates who can question everything they are told to do.
Organisations become fragile because graduates are too well trained.
Workers who can only follow instructions, not think critically, make organisations vulnerable.
Training graduates to ask questions makes them unwilling to work hard.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Trained to execute but not to question' means capable of following procedures but not of critical thinking; such workers make organisations brittle when situations change.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Execute' means carry out instructions; 'question' means think critically.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which opening sentence best avoids generic filler and uses concrete data?
options: In today's world, many people wonder whether studying the humanities is worthwhile.
It is a well-known fact that university funding has changed in recent years.
Between 2012 and 2022, the number of students majoring in English at US universities fell by nearly thirty per cent.
Throughout history, people have valued both practical and theoretical education.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C opens with a specific time range, a specific country, a specific subject, and a specific percentage — no vague framing needed.
mcQuestions.hint: Date range + country + subject + percentage beats every generic opener.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition correctly introduces a concession at B2 level?
options: Granted, the case for vocational alignment has genuine force.
Even so, the case for vocational alignment has genuine force.
Furthermore, the case for vocational alignment has genuine force.
In contrast, the case for vocational alignment has genuine force.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' introduces a concession — admitting the opposing view has merit before pivoting back. 'Even so' would pivot away from a concession, not introduce one.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Granted' introduces the concession; 'Even so' would follow it.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be crowded out' mean in an educational context?
options: To become more popular than competing subjects
To be gradually displaced and given less space by other priorities
To attract more students than a building can hold
To be officially banned by the government
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Crowded out' means displaced by something that takes up more space or priority — humanities being pushed aside by STEM and vocational subjects in funding and prestige.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what happens when too many things compete for a limited space.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains a formal B2 register throughout?
options: Studying philosophy is literally useless and no one needs it for a job.
Like, nobody hires philosophers so why bother studying it tbh.
What is at stake is not the survival of any particular department but the idea that a person can be educated — not merely trained.
The humanities are kinda important but you can't really make money with them so it's complicated.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise vocabulary ('at stake', 'educated', 'merely') and a measured, analytical tone with no slang.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, when are humanities skills 'deployed', even if they don't appear on job listings?
options: Only in academic settings when writing research papers
Every time a manager chooses a strategy, a lawyer reads a contract, or a citizen votes
Only when people are explicitly asked to think philosophically
Only in countries with strong liberal arts traditions
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly states these skills are 'deployed every time a manager chooses a strategy, a lawyer reads a contract, or a citizen votes'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the list of three professional situations in the fourth paragraph.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If you were designing a university curriculum from scratch, what proportion of it would be devoted to subjects with no direct commercial application — and what would you be willing to give up to make space for them?
